title: SEGMENT 16 — CMC — chemistry, manufacturing, and controls, the limiting step

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — CMC — chemistry, manufacturing, and controls, the limiting step
I have been promising for several segments now that we would come back to CMC, and here we are.
Chemistry, manufacturing, and controls.
The unglamorous part of the regulatory file.
The part nobody writes news articles about.
And, in my experience, the part that more gene therapy programs trip over than any other single component.

CMC, as a regulatory acronym, refers to the section of any drug application that describes how the drug is made, what its physical and chemical properties are, what quality testing it undergoes, and what manufacturing controls ensure that every batch is the same.

For a small-molecule drug, the CMC section is substantial but reasonably tractable — chemistry has been a precise science for two centuries, and the tools for characterizing organic compounds are mature.
For a gene therapy, CMC is harder.
Much harder.
Because you are not making a defined small molecule.
You are making a viral particle, in a living cell culture, with all the variability that living systems introduce.
Every batch of AAV is, at some level, a population of slightly different particles.
Some have empty capsids — no DNA inside.
Some have full capsids with the right DNA.
Some have full capsids with the wrong DNA — fragments, host DNA contamination, weird recombinants.
Some have aggregates.
Some have post-translational modifications that vary from batch to batch.
The job of CMC is to characterize all of this, quantify it, control it, and demonstrate that each batch meets specifications consistent enough to be a reliable drug.
Let me give you the analogy I want you to hold.
CMC is the unglamorous gating step — making your gene therapy at clinical scale, with documented purity and consistency, often takes longer than the biology.

I have known gene therapy programs where the science was completely done — the vector was designed, the preclinical work was complete, the clinical protocol was ready — and the entire program was held up for months or years because the manufacturing was not yet at the standard the agency required.
The science said go.
The manufacturing said wait.
The specific problems in gene therapy CMC are several.
Let me walk through them.
First, scale.
The doses required for gene therapy — particularly for systemic AAV — are enormous.
A single Zolgensma dose contains on the order of ten to the fourteenth vector genomes.
Manufacturing enough vector to dose a clinical trial cohort, much less a commercial population, requires bioreactors and downstream processing at substantial scale.
The field has been chronically capacity-limited; for years, there were simply not enough GMP-compliant AAV manufacturing slots to keep up with demand.
Second, characterization.

Demonstrating that your AAV product is what you say it is requires a battery of analytical methods — quantification of full versus empty capsids by analytical ultracentrifugation or charge-detection mass spectrometry, characterization of the packaged DNA by sequencing, measurement of potency in a cell-based assay, profiling of host cell protein and DNA contaminants, and so on.
Each of these methods has to be validated to GMP standards.
The validation packages themselves are enormous.
Third, consistency.
The agency wants to see that batch-to-batch variability is small and well-controlled.
You demonstrate this by making multiple batches over time and showing that the analytical results fall within specified ranges.
Establishing those ranges, and producing the consistent batches, takes time.
Fourth, GMP — Good Manufacturing Practice — compliance.
Every step of the manufacturing process has to be documented in detail, conducted in a controlled environment, and subject to formal change control.
Facilities have to be designed to GMP standards.
Personnel have to be trained.
Quality systems have to be in place.
Building or contracting for a GMP AAV manufacturing capability is, for many small biotech sponsors, the single largest capital and operational commitment in their entire development program.
For some gene therapy programs, the manufacturing complexity has been more limiting than the biology.
There have been clinical trials delayed for years waiting for manufacturing capacity.
There have been programs that succeeded scientifically but failed commercially because they could not produce the drug at a cost that allowed for sustainable pricing.
There have been BLAs that received complete response letters — the FDA's formal "we need more from you" — primarily because of CMC concerns rather than safety or efficacy concerns.
The field has been working on this.
The growth of dedicated AAV contract manufacturing organizations — Catalent, Lonza, ThermoFisher, and others — has expanded the available capacity.
New cell line technologies and production platforms are increasing yields.
Analytical methods are maturing.
But CMC remains a chronic bottleneck, and any gene therapy program that does not start serious investment in manufacturing at the same time as the biology will, in my observation, hit a wall later.
The manufacturing has to develop in parallel with the science.
If it doesn't, your drug will be ready and you will have nothing to put in a syringe.
